turns.content: Okay so — *[laughs a little, settling in]* — I keep falling for emotionally unavailable people. Like, it's basically a curse at this point. Third relationship, same exact configuration, and I'm sitting here going... *why do I always end up with people who just cannot show up?*
That laugh at the start — what's in it?

*[slight pause, then waves a hand]*

Oh, it's just — you know, gallows humor? Like, if I don't laugh at how absurd it is, I'll just be depressed about it. Three times! Same guy, different face. It's almost impressive, honestly.

*[leans forward slightly]*

But that's kind of why I'm here, right? Because clearly there's something I'm not seeing. Some pattern in who I'm *picking*. Like, is there a sign I'm missing? Do I need to just... run the other direction when someone seems a little mysterious and brooding? *[eye roll]* Because apparently that's my type and my type is terrible.
What happens when someone is just... clearly into you?

*[quick smile, a little too quick]*

I mean — that's what I *want*, right? That's the whole thing. I want someone who actually shows up, who's present, who's like... *there*. 

*[shifts slightly]*

I've had that, kind of. Like early on, before things get complicated, there's usually a phase where they seem really into it. And then something shifts and suddenly I'm the one texting first every time and wondering if I said something wrong and—

*[catches herself]*

But yeah, no, someone being clearly into me would be — that would be great. That's the dream. Why, do you think that's the issue? Like I'm somehow repelling the available ones?

I noticed you didn't quite answer the question.

Not "what do you want" — what actually *happens* in your body when someone is clearly, unambiguously into you. Like, right now, if you imagine it.

*[another quick laugh, smaller this time]*

I mean... it feels good? Obviously? Like, that's — 

*[pauses, looks slightly to the side]*

I don't know, it's hard to imagine because it's been a while since someone was just... straightforwardly like that. Without the — without me having to read between the lines constantly.

*[a beat, then gets a little more animated, slightly deflecting]*

Actually you know what's funny, my last ex — in the beginning he was pretty attentive, and I remember thinking "okay, this is it, this is different." And then gradually it just... evaporated. So maybe the question is less about what happens in my body and more about why they always *stop*. Like what's the pattern there, because that seems like the more useful thing to figure out, right?

*[bright, expectant look, like she's handed the coach a better question to work with]*

You just did something interesting. I asked what happens in your body, and you handed me a different question to answer.

What's happening right now, as you sit with the original one?

*[small, caught smile — a little defensive underneath it]*

I mean... I redirected, yeah. I heard it.

*[quieter, slower]*

I just — when I actually try to sit with it, it's kind of... I don't know. It goes a little blank? Like I can tell you intellectually that it would be wonderful, I can describe what it would look like from the outside, but the actual felt sense of it is...

*[trails off, taps fingers on knee once]*

It's weird. It's like trying to picture a color I haven't seen.

*[quick recovery, lighter tone]*

Which is probably just because I haven't experienced it properly, right? Like my reference points are all these unavailable guys so of course I can't quite — I'm sure once it actually happened I'd just... receive it. Totally fine. 

*[the "totally fine" lands a little hollow even as she says it]*
That "totally fine" — did you hear it as it came out?

*[pause, then a short exhale that's almost a laugh but not quite]*

Yeah. Yeah, I heard it.

*[looks down for a second, then back up]*

It sounded... defensive. Which is annoying because there's nothing to be defensive about, it's a hypothetical.

*[quieter]*

I don't know why that question keeps making me want to change the subject. It's not — it's not a scary question. Someone loving me openly isn't a scary thing to want.

*[but she's gone a little still, which is different from the fast, expressive energy of before]*
The stillness right now — what's in it?

*[long pause, longer than anything before]*

*[looks away briefly]*

I don't... 

*[small laugh that doesn't quite land as humor]*

It's like there's something right at the edge of it that I don't — I'm not sure I want to look at directly.

*[quieter, more careful]*

Because the chasing thing, right, that's exhausting and it's clearly not working, but it's also — it's *known*. I know how to do that. I know how to be the one trying, I know how to read someone who's hard to read, I know how to make myself useful in that configuration.

*[pause]*

And I'm sitting here and I can't actually picture... just being received. Like someone just — seeing me and wanting to be there. Without me having to earn it or maintain it or—

*[stops herself, shifts slightly, voice goes a little brighter as if to close the door she just opened]*

I mean that's just — that's an attachment thing, right? Probably anxious attachment or whatever. I've read about this.

Stay with what you just said before the attachment thing.

"Without me having to earn it."

What happens in your body when you say that?

*[very still]*

*[long silence, longer than she's been comfortable with this whole conversation]*

*[when she speaks it's quieter, slower, like she's actually checking]*

There's something... tight. In my chest. Like a—

*[small, almost involuntary laugh, but it's different from the earlier ones — there's something raw in it]*

That's — huh.

*[pause]*

I was going to say "that's weird" and move on but—

*[voice drops]*

It's like the idea of it is almost — it makes me want to cry a little? Which doesn't make sense because it's supposed to be the good thing. It's the thing I say I want.

*[looks up, something genuinely confused and a little exposed in her face]*

Why would that make me want to cry?

payload.seed_rationale: Client presents the anxious-avoidant dynamic pattern across three relationships with emotionally unavailable partners; the repetition signals both a stuck pattern and complementary trauma matching. The laugh and "curse" framing suggest intellectual awareness without felt understanding of the underlying mechanism.
bundle_reasons: This file directly names the client's pattern (third relationship, same configuration, chasing emotionally unavailable people) and maps the coaching path Joe would take in this moment.

Client is explicitly stuck in a repeating pattern ("third relationship, same exact configuration") and asking "why do I always" — this file maps the exact coaching move Joe makes when a behavior is recursive and a limiting belief is operating underneath.

This file directly explains why the client keeps selecting emotionally unavailable partners—the trauma click mechanism—and maps the precise interlocking that makes the pattern feel inevitable and repeatable.

This file directly maps the anxious-avoidant dynamic the client is stuck in—chasing emotionally unavailable people—and explains the mechanism (chasing pushes away) and the avoided emotional states (receiving love, being adored) that keep the pattern alive.

decision.bundle_reason: This file addresses fear of losing oneself in love, but the client's presenting pattern is about chasing emotionally unavailable people—a complementary-trauma selection mechanism, not a fear-of-intimacy avoidance pattern. Already covered by bundle files.
decision.follow_reason: These edges explore the specific imprints and boundary work that underlie why the client keeps selecting unavailable partners—the healthy vs. unhealthy loss-of-self distinction and inherited beliefs about love are relevant to the deeper pattern.

decision.follow_reason: These edges point to the actual blockers underneath the client's repeating pattern—the limiting beliefs, the hidden want, the shame, and the pattern's protective intent—which Joe would explore once the client recognizes the pattern itself.

The gallows humor and eye-roll suggest a happiness filter masking deeper frustration. The "what am I not seeing" points to a blind spot/same-wall pattern.

decision.bundle_reason: The happiness filter is a body-read tool, but the client hasn't shown the diagnostic signal (fixed pleasant smile masking real feeling); their laugh is self-aware gallows humor, not an unexamined mask.
decision.follow_reason: These edges explore the deeper beliefs driving partner selection and avoidance patterns—trust, loyalty bonds, and what the client is actually fleeing—which are the real levers for this pattern work.

decision.follow_reason: These three edges connect the client's pattern to the specific childhood imprints and boundary work that would unlock why she picks unavailable partners—the real target Joe needs.

decision.bundle_reason: Too general/hub-like: this is a meta-principle about methodology, not the specific blocker (anxious attachment, avoidance of being loved) that Joe needs to address in this turn.
decision.follow_reason: Client is asking "what am I not seeing?" and "is there a sign I'm missing?"—these edges map the actual blockers (limiting beliefs), the function of the pattern (intent), and the want underneath the surface goal (picking partners) that Joe will need to surface.

payload.seed_rationale: Client deflects from somatic inquiry about receiving love by intellectualizing the pattern; shows classic anxious-avoidant dynamic (they pursue then withdraw); demonstrates resistance to feeling the actual body sensation of being loved, redirecting to "why do they leave" narrative instead of examining her own reception/rejection of the welcome.
bundle_reasons: Client is actively demonstrating the pattern: deflecting from body sensation, reframing the question to blame partners for "stopping," and showing the exact victim-frame Joe needs to interrupt first.

Client is actively deflecting from body sensation ("it feels good? obviously?") and reframing away from her own reception—this read directly names the pattern Joe needs to interrupt: the flash of not-letting-in that she's performing right now.

This file names the exact configuration Joe is interrupting: client chases unavailable partners, experiences herself as the wanting one, and deflects from the avoided states (receiving, being adored). The file directly maps her pattern and the work required.

Client is actively deflecting the sensation of being received ("it feels good? obviously?") and reframing away from her own body—this move names exactly what Joe needs her to do: stop blocking and let the recognition/love move through without the quick laugh and subject change.

decision.bundle_reason: Client is actively demonstrating the pattern: deflecting from body sensation, reframing the question to blame partners for "stopping," and showing the exact victim-frame Joe needs to interrupt first.

decision.bundle_reason: Client is actively deflecting from body sensation ("it feels good? obviously?") and reframing away from her own reception—this read directly names the pattern Joe needs to interrupt: the flash of not-letting-in that she's performing right now.
decision.follow_reason: Client needs to understand what happens when she *does* let something in (welcoming), and what blocks the flow when she doesn't (the channel work)—both are directly relevant to her pattern of partners "stopping."

decision.bundle_reason: This file names the exact configuration Joe is interrupting: client chases unavailable partners, experiences herself as the wanting one, and deflects from the avoided states (receiving, being adored). The file directly maps her pattern and the work required.
decision.follow_reason: These edges name the specific mechanisms Joe needs: how her wanting pushes away, what emotional states she's avoiding (the file names them), why grief matters to break the cycle, and how her partner choice is precise—all directly relevant to interrupting her victim frame and showing her agency.

decision.bundle_reason: This file names a deeper fear (loss of self) that may underlie her pattern, but she's not yet accessing that fear—she's still in deflection and blame-externalization. Adding it now would skip the immediate work Joe needs to do: interrupt the deflection and get her into body sensation first.
decision.follow_reason: These three edges are waypoints for later in the session: once Joe gets her to feel what happens when someone is into her, these files will help name what she's actually protecting against (boundary collapse, parental repetition, the cost of love). Don't follow the push-pull or transactional edges yet—she hasn't named her own role in the pattern.

decision.bundle_reason: This file teaches welcoming a partner's difficult emotion; the client's immediate work is recognizing her own deflection from receiving welcome, not yet managing a partner's anger.
decision.follow_reason: These edges map the caretaking pattern she's enacting (managing the partner's "stopping" rather than feeling her own reception), the distinction between validating emotion vs. story (her deflection), and the direct intervention Joe might use to interrupt her reframing.

decision.bundle_reason: Client is actively deflecting the sensation of being received ("it feels good? obviously?") and reframing away from her own body—this move names exactly what Joe needs her to do: stop blocking and let the recognition/love move through without the quick laugh and subject change.
decision.follow_reason: Both are direct applications of the hollow-bone move to her specific pattern: the first operationalizes receiving love from a partner (her stated want), the second shows how to receive without the deflection-as-defense that she's performing right now.
